skill.name: statistical-analysis-advisor
skill.description: Recommends appropriate statistical methods (T-test vs ANOVA, etc.) based on data type, group structure, and distribution assumptions.
skill.summary: Intelligent statistical test recommendation engine with assumption checking and power analysis.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 147 static findings are false positives. The external_commands findings (105 locations) are Markdown code fence backticks in documentation, not Ruby shell backtick execution. The weak cryptographic algorithm findings (38 locations) are statistical terminology (ANOVA, T-test, Chi-Square) misidentified as cryptographic references. Path traversal findings are security checklist items documenting defenses. The only true positive is a hardcoded GitHub URL to the skill's own source repository, which is benign. The skill is a legitimate statistical analysis advisor.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded GitHub repository URL
low_findings.description: SKILL.md contains a hardcoded URL to the skill's GitHub repository. This is the skill's own source repository link used for attribution. No data exfiltration risk. Low severity.

dangerous_patterns.title: Markdown code blocks in reference documentation
dangerous_patterns.description: Static scanner flagged Markdown code fence backticks as Ruby shell backtick execution. These are code examples (Python, R, bash) in documentation files. No actual shell injection risk. 105 locations across SKILL.md and reference files.

content.user_title: Find the Right Statistical Test for Your Data
content.value_statement: Choosing the wrong statistical test can invalidate your research findings. This skill analyzes your data characteristics to recommend the correct statistical method with assumption checks and power analysis.
actual_capabilities: Recommends appropriate statistical tests based on data type, group structure, and distribution assumptions
Checks key assumptions including normality, homogeneity of variance, and independence of observations
Calculates statistical power and required sample sizes for study design planning
Provides alternative test suggestions when assumptions are violated
Compares parametric, non-parametric, and robust test options for your scenario
Interprets effect size magnitudes using Cohen's d, eta-squared, and Cramer's V
limitations: Does not perform actual data analysis or compute test statistics from raw data
Cannot access raw data files directly to analyze them
Complex multivariate designs may require specialized statistical consultation
Bayesian analysis methods are not covered by this skill

use_cases.title: Study Design and Methodology Planning
use_cases.description: A graduate researcher planning a clinical trial needs to determine the correct statistical test for their study design. They provide their data type, number of groups, and distribution assumptions to receive a test recommendation with sample size requirements.
use_cases.target_user: Researchers and graduate students planning studies

use_cases.title: Statistical Methodology Validation
use_cases.description: A data analyst has prepared a statistical analysis plan and wants to verify their test choices are appropriate. They use the skill to confirm their selected tests and identify any assumption violations that need to be addressed.

use_cases.title: Peer Review and Methodology Verification
use_cases.description: A journal reviewer evaluating a manuscript checks whether the authors selected appropriate statistical methods for their reported data types and study design. The skill helps identify potential methodological issues.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Test Selection
prompt_templates.prompt: I have [continuous/categorical/ordinal] data with [number] groups. The samples are [independent/paired]. The distribution appears [normal/non-normal/unknown]. What statistical test should I use?
prompt_templates.scenario: Quick test recommendation when you know your data characteristics

prompt_templates.title: Assumption Checking Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: I plan to use [test type, e.g., independent t-test]. My sample sizes per group are [values]. The normality test p-values are [values]. The Levene test p-value is [value]. Check whether my assumptions are satisfied.
prompt_templates.scenario: Verifying assumptions before running a planned statistical test

prompt_templates.title: Power Analysis and Sample Size
prompt_templates.prompt: I expect an effect size of [value] with alpha [value]. I have [number] groups. How many participants do I need per group to achieve [80/90/95] percent power? Or: I have [number] participants per group. What power can I achieve with effect size [value]?
prompt_templates.scenario: Planning sample sizes or evaluating achieved statistical power

prompt_templates.title: Advanced Test Comparison
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare parametric, non-parametric, and robust test options for my scenario. My data is [type] with [number] groups, [independent/paired] samples, and [known/unknown] distribution. Show me the trade-offs between approaches.
prompt_templates.scenario: Exploring multiple valid approaches for complex data situations

output_examples.input: I have continuous data with 2 independent groups and a normal distribution. What test should I use?
output_examples.output:
Recommended Test: Independent Samples T-Test

Rationale: Selected Independent Samples T-Test based on continuous data type, 2 independent groups, and normal distribution.

Alternative Tests: Welch's T-Test (if variances unequal), Mann-Whitney U Test (if normality violated), Yuen's Trimmed Mean T-Test (robust alternative)

Key Assumptions:
1. Independence of observations
2. Normality of each group (robust with n > 30 per group)
3. Homogeneity of variance (check with Levene's test)

Warning: Minimum n = 20 per group recommended for robust normality tests.

output_examples.input: I have an effect size of 0.5 (Cohen's d), alpha of 0.05, and 30 participants per group. What power do I have?
output_examples.output:
Power Analysis Results:

Effect Size: 0.50 (Medium)
Sample Size: 30 per group
Alpha: 0.05
Achieved Power: Approximately 48%

Interpretation: This study has approximately 48% power to detect a medium effect. This is below the conventional 80% threshold. Consider increasing your sample size to approximately 64 participants per group to achieve 80% power.

output_examples.input: What is considered a large effect size for a chi-square test?
output_examples.output:
Effect Size Interpretation for Cramers V:

Small: 0.1
Medium: 0.3
Large: 0.5
Very Large: Above 0.5

For a chi-square test with a 2x2 table, a Cramers V of 0.5 or higher is considered a large effect. This means the association between your categorical variables is strong and likely to be practically significant.

best_practices: Verify all statistical recommendations with domain-specific knowledge and consult a statistician for critical research decisions
Provide complete and accurate information about your data characteristics including distribution type and sample relationships
Run assumption checks before finalizing your test choice to identify violations early
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill to perform statistical calculations or analysis on raw data values
Do not assume recommendations apply without verifying the underlying assumptions for your specific dataset
Do not rely solely on this skill for regulatory submissions, clinical trial decisions, or other high-stakes research without expert review

faq.question: Can this skill run statistical tests on my data?
faq.answer: No. This skill provides recommendations only. It does not compute test statistics, p-values, or analyze raw data.

faq.question: What information do I need to provide?
faq.answer: You need to specify your data type (continuous, categorical, ordinal), number of groups, whether samples are independent or paired, and your distribution assumption (normal, non-normal, unknown).

faq.question: How is this different from using Python stats libraries directly?
faq.answer: This skill acts as a guidance layer above statistical libraries. It helps you select the right test before running any code, and explains the reasoning behind each recommendation.

faq.question: Can I use this for clinical or medical research?
faq.answer: This skill is advisory only. All recommendations require human verification by a qualified biostatistician before application in clinical research.

faq.question: Does it support Bayesian statistical methods?
faq.answer: No. This skill focuses on frequentist statistical methods. Bayesian analysis is not currently covered.

faq.question: What if my data violates multiple assumptions?
faq.answer: The skill suggests alternative tests when assumptions are violated. For complex violations, it recommends consulting a statistician for specialized guidance.
